event: Eve and the Tree of Knowledge
symbols: red fruit — the forbidden fruit, the object of temptation and desire
serpent winding through branches — the tempter who deceives Eve
hand reaching for fruit — the fateful choice that inaugurates the fall
green and red intertwined — the entanglement of life and death, innocence and sin
butterfly — the fleeting beauty of Eden about to be lost
abstract face merged with foliage — Eve's identity intertwined with the garden itself
description: An abstract painting in vivid green, red, and black showing a stylized female face merged with organic foliage and serpentine forms. A hand reaches toward a round red fruit on a branch at upper left. A snake-like green form winds through the right side of the composition. A butterfly hovers near the top. The entire image is a dense interweaving of organic forms — leaves, branches, faces, and sinuous lines — all rendered in bold contrasting colors that create a sense of entangled, pulsing life.
theological_description: This vivid, expressionistic depiction of Eve reaching for the forbidden fruit captures the moment Genesis describes as the origin of human sin and mortality. The serpent, 'more crafty than any other wild animal that the Lord God had made' (Genesis 3:1), insinuated doubt about God's command and desire for the fruit that was 'good for food, pleasing to the eye, and desirable for gaining wisdom' (Genesis 3:6). The abstract interweaving of Eve's form with the garden foliage suggests the original harmony between humanity and creation that is about to be shattered. The reaching hand is the gesture that changes everything — the assertion of autonomous will over divine command. The serpent's sinuous presence throughout the composition suggests that temptation is not external but woven into the fabric of the fallen world. The vivid red of the fruit — the color of both desire and danger — draws the eye just as it drew Eve's. The theological tradition reads this scene as the felix culpa, the 'happy fault' that occasioned the greater gift of redemption in Christ, the second Adam whose obedience would undo what the first couple's disobedience inaugurated.

scripture_refs.reason: The serpent's temptation and Eve's taking of the forbidden fruit — the central scene depicted
God's command not to eat from the tree of knowledge of good and evil, under penalty of death
Through one person sin entered the world, and through sin death — Paul's theology of the fall
The ancient serpent identified as the devil and Satan who deceives the whole world
Paul's reference to Eve being deceived by the serpent's cunning
